literal: strand, cord, constituent (root sense: a strand of a rope); in later technical usage 'quality, attribute', but the Sāṃkhya register preserves the strand-of-prakṛti sense and the Vaiśeṣika register the property-of-substance sense — these are different metaphysical registers that English flattens
invariant_definition:
A *guṇa* is not 'a quality' in the Western analytic sense of an accidental predicate inhering in a substance. Two distinct technical registers must be held apart, because the Vedāntic schools work primarily within the first while continuing to read texts that operate in the second.

(1) **Sāṃkhya register (the dominant Vedāntic register).** The three *guṇas* — *sattva* (lucid, ordered, illuminating), *rajas* (active, energizing, passion-producing), *tamas* (heavy, obscuring, inertial) — are the three ontological strands or constituent fabrics of *prakṛti* itself. They are not properties *of* the world; they are what the world is made of. *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* 11–13 describes their joint operation under the rope-strand metaphor: the manifest is woven from three strands that mutually suppress and support one another, the dominance-ratio at any locus determining the character of that locus. The *guṇas* are *bhautika* in a strict sense — substantial constituents, not adjectives. This is why *guṇa-traya*, *traiguṇya*, *guṇātīta*, *nirguṇa*, *saguṇa* all carry weight that 'three qualities', 'tri-quality', 'beyond qualities', 'without qualities', 'with qualities' cannot bear: the Sanskrit terms reach into the constitutive metaphysics of *prakṛti*, while the English glosses suggest mere predicate-logic.

(2) **Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika register.** *Guṇa* is one of the six (or seven) *padārtha*s of Vaiśeṣika categorial ontology — a real but dependent property-substance inhering by *samavāya* in *dravya*. Here 'quality' is a defensible English gloss because the Vaiśeṣika *padārtha*-grid is itself a property-of-substance scheme. The twenty-four *guṇa*s of classical Vaiśeṣika (color, taste, smell, touch, number, magnitude, separateness, conjunction, disjunction, priority, posteriority, weight, fluidity, viscidity, sound, *buddhi*, *sukha*, *duḥkha*, *icchā*, *dveṣa*, *prayatna*, *dharma*, *adharma*, *saṃskāra*) are inhering properties, not constitutive strands.

In Vedānta the two registers are constantly in play. When a school says *brahman* is *nirguṇa*, the question 'in which register?' is doctrinally decisive — see the entries on *nirguṇa* and *saguṇa* for the sub-school analysis.

per_school.definition: The three *guṇas* are the constituents of *prakṛti*; their *sāmya-avasthā* (state of equipoise) is *avyakta* (unmanifest *prakṛti*), and their disequilibrium initiates manifestation through *mahat*, *ahaṅkāra*, and the rest of the twenty-four evolutes. *Puruṣa* is *guṇātīta* — beyond the *guṇa*s — but is not 'attributeless' in any predicate-logic sense; *puruṣa* is constitutively outside the *prakṛti*-strand-fabric.

The Sāṃkhya *guṇas* are admitted at the *vyāvahārika* level as constituents of *prakṛti*-as-*māyā*. *Sattva*-pradominance in the *antaḥkaraṇa* is the cognitive pre-condition for *brahma-jñāna*. Crucially, when Advaita calls *brahman* *nirguṇa*, two things are meant simultaneously: (a) *brahman* is *guṇātīta* in the Sāṃkhya sense — beyond the three-strand fabric of *prakṛti*; (b) *brahman* is *nirupādhika* — free of all *upādhi*-determined predicates that arise only at the *vyāvahārika* standpoint. The two senses coincide for Advaita because *upādhi* and *guṇa-māyā* are functionally one. *Brahman* is not 'qualityless' in a logical-positive sense; it is the substrate that the strand-and-upādhi grammar simply does not reach.

Two distinct uses. (i) The Sāṃkhya three-strand *guṇa*s constitute *acit* — real and eternally so; *miśra-sattva* characterizes the bound *jīva*'s embodiment, *śuddha-sattva* the eternal substance of Vaikuṇṭha and the bodies of the Lord and the *muktas*. (ii) *Brahman*'s *kalyāṇa-guṇa*s — the infinite auspicious *guṇa*s (*ananta-kalyāṇa-guṇa*) — are *not* Sāṃkhya-strand *guṇa*s but essential, eternal, real attributes constitutive of the Lord's *svarūpa*. *Nirguṇa* texts deny only *heya-guṇa*s (defects) and *prākṛta-guṇa*s (the three-strand fabric); the *kalyāṇa-guṇa*s are eternally affirmed.

The three-strand *guṇa*s of *prakṛti* are real constituents — but, unlike in Viśiṣṭādvaita, they are tied to the *taratamya* of souls: the *mukti-yogya*, *nitya-saṃsārī*, and *tamo-yogya* classes are constitutionally aligned with *sāttvika*, *rājasa*, and *tāmasa* affinities respectively. Viṣṇu possesses infinite real *kalyāṇa-guṇa*s; *nirguṇa* texts deny only *prākṛta-guṇa*s.

*Guṇa* is one of the *padārtha*s — a property-substance inhering by *samavāya* in *dravya*. Twenty-four *guṇa*s are enumerated. Here 'quality' is a defensible gloss because the categorial scheme is itself a substance-and-property scheme; the Sāṃkhya tri-strand metaphysics is absent.

The three *guṇa*s appear within the *prakṛti-tattva* and below in the thirty-six-tattva scheme; they are the constituents of the lower manifest order under Śiva's *svātantrya*. They are not the highest metaphysical principle and do not exhaust the real, but their reality at their own level is preserved.

translator_note: Translating *guṇa* uniformly as 'quality' is the foundational lazy collapse from which a chain of downstream errors follows. The Sāṃkhya-derived register — where *guṇa* names a constitutive strand of *prakṛti* — is the dominant Vedāntic register. In that register *guṇa* is no more a 'quality' than a strand of a rope is a 'quality' of the rope. When the gloss 'quality' is then carried into *nirguṇa* and *saguṇa*, the resulting English ('without qualities', 'with qualities') reads as a flat predicate-logic claim and severs the term from the metaphysics it lives in. Acceptable practice: leave *guṇa* untranslated; on first use specify the register (Sāṃkhya-strand vs. Vaiśeṣika-padārtha vs. Vaiṣṇava-kalyāṇa); never translate *guṇātīta* as 'beyond qualities' (use 'beyond the three *guṇa*s' or 'beyond the *prakṛti*-strands'); never gloss *nirguṇa* as 'qualityless' or *saguṇa* as 'qualified' without qualification.
